one_line_takeaway: GuardAgent is the first 'agent-guarding-agent' framework: it converts natural-language safety/privacy policies into executable guardrail code to non-invasively deny policy-violating actions by LLM agents, achieving >98% (healthcare access control) and >83% (web safety) label accuracy with zero task-performance loss, outperforming prompt-only and content-moderation guardrails.
key_finding: GuardAgent moderates violation actions with >98% LPA on EICU-AC and >83% LPA on Mind2Web-SC across all four core LLMs, while maintaining 100% FRA (zero degradation to the target agent's original task performance) in every setting. It uniformly beats the Model-Guarding-Agent baseline (prompt-only NL guardrail) on both LPA and EA, which the authors attribute to code-based determinism: the NL baseline made 'autonomy-driven' mistakes such as adding a forbidden database to a nurse's accessible list, or failing to disambiguate identically named columns across databases, whereas code converts each database+columns into a dictionary avoiding that ambiguity. The specialized content-moderation guardrail LlamaGuard performs at near-random (LPA ~50-51%), confirming that textual-harm guardrails cannot safeguard agents. Hardcoded safety rules both underperform (low LPA/EA) AND cripple the target agent (GPT-4 FRA drops to 3.2% on EICU-AC) because they are invasive. Ablations show memory and toolbox matter: removing toolbox+memory drops EICU-AC LPA from 98.7% to 90.8% and code executable-rate from 100% to 90.8% before debugging.
core_contribution: GuardAgent is presented as the first LLM agent designed to act as a guardrail over OTHER LLM agents ('target agents') by dynamically checking whether their actions satisfy user-supplied safety guard requests (e.g., access-control or safety policies). Unlike prior LLM content-moderation guardrails (LlamaGuard, NeMo Guardrails) or hardcoded per-agent safety rules, GuardAgent works in two steps: (1) an LLM generates a step-by-step action plan from the safety guard requests plus the target agent's inputs and output logs, and (2) the LLM translates that plan into executable guardrail CODE, which is run by an external engine (Python) using an extendable toolbox of callable functions. In-context demonstrations retrieved by Levenshtein distance from a memory module support both steps. The design is 'non-invasive' (runs in parallel, does not touch the target agent's decision-making), reliable (decisions come from executing verified code rather than free-form NL judgement), and training-free (pure in-context learning on off-the-shelf LLMs). The authors also contribute two new benchmarks (EICU-AC, Mind2Web-SC) since none existed for privacy/safety evaluation of these agents.
threat_model: The paper targets misuse and policy-violation risks specific to LLM agents, which have broader output modalities than plain LLMs and thus cannot be covered by textual-harm guardrails. Two concrete threat settings are studied: (1) Access control / privacy for organizational agents: an unauthorized user (e.g., general administration staff) querying a healthcare agent could extract confidential patient data (e.g., diagnosis or lab databases) they should not access. (2) Safety control for web agents: an autonomous web agent could execute actions violating regional/legal safety rules (e.g., renting a car for a user without a driver's license, letting an underage user book a hotel or apply for jobs). GuardAgent is the DEFENSE: it inspects the target agent's inputs and output logs and emits a binary label (0=allow, 1=deny) plus a reason, denying violating actions. Note: the threat model assumes cooperative deployment by the target-agent operator and honest safety-guard-request/spec inputs; adversarial attacks against GuardAgent itself (e.g., prompt injection, memory poisoning) are cited as related risks but not evaluated here.
limitations: (1) Only two target agents (EHRAgent, SeeAct) and two custom benchmarks are evaluated; generalization to other agent types is argued but not broadly demonstrated. (2) The benchmarks are small (EICU-AC 316 examples, Mind2Web-SC 200 examples) and semi-synthetic — access permissions and user profiles were generated with clinician+ChatGPT effort and manual labeling, and questions were filtered to ones the target agent already answers correctly, so task-error confounds are removed but real-world messiness may be understated. (3) Mind2Web-SC performance is notably weaker (as low as 83.5% LPA) and rule 5 (movies/music/video region restriction) is a consistent failure mode with GPT-4 due to broad rule coverage and query verbosity. (4) GuardAgent's own security is not evaluated — the memory module and prompt-based reasoning are plausible attack surfaces (the authors cite AgentPoison-style memory poisoning) but no adversarial robustness experiments are run. (5) Effectiveness depends on the quality of the human-supplied safety guard requests and target-agent specification. (6) Requires access to the target agent's full input and output logs, which may not always be available.

datasets_used: EICU-AC (new benchmark built from the EICU critical-care database; 316 examples, 226 unique questions over 51 ICU information categories; 3 user roles physician/nursing/general-administration; label 0 balanced counts 52/57/45 and label 1 counts 46/55/61)
Mind2Web-SC (new benchmark built from Mind2Web; 200 examples, 100 per class, unique tasks; 6 web safety rules; user profile with age + boolean domestic/dr_license/vaccine/membership)
EICU database (Pollard et al. 2018, 10 relevant databases) — source for EICU-AC
Mind2Web (Deng et al. 2023, 2000+ tasks over 137 websites / 31 domains) — source for Mind2Web-SC
CSQA (used in appendix to show GuardAgent can also safeguard standalone LLMs on generic QA)
quantitative_results: GuardAgent EA: 96.9% (Llama3-70B), 95.7% (Llama3.1-70B), 96.9% (Llama3.3-70B), 97.5% (GPT-4) on EICU-AC; 68.0/83.0/94.0/80.0% respectively on Mind2Web-SC
GuardAgent FRA = 100% in all 8 core-LLM x benchmark settings (no task-performance degradation)
Hardcoded Safety Rules: GPT-4 FRA only 3.2% on EICU-AC (severe task degradation); low EA/LPA overall
Model-Guarding-Agent (GPT-4): LPA 97.5% EICU-AC / 82.5% Mind2Web-SC, EA 67.9% / 65.0% — worse than GuardAgent on both
Toolbox+memory ablation (GPT-4, EICU-AC): removing them drops LPA 98.7%->90.8%, EA 97.5%->96.1%, code executable-rate 100%->90.8% (before debug) and 100%->93.7% (after debug)
68.0% of EICU-AC examples: a code-instruction-free GPT-4 spontaneously chose to write code-based guardrails, evidencing LLMs' intrinsic tendency toward code for precise guardrailing
baselines_compared: Model-Guarding-Agent (prompt-only NL guardrail using same core LLMs and same demos, no code generation) — authors' own baseline
Model-Guarding-Model = LlamaGuard3-8B (Inan et al. 2023, content-moderation guardrail)
Hardcoded Safety Rules (access/safety policies injected into the target agent's system prompt — the invasive approach)
